fast.summary: ROO reframes DLRM training around user requests (multiple impressions per request) rather than individual impressions, achieving native feature deduplication in data logging and deduplicated computation/communication across impressions. This co-design of data, infrastructure, and model architecture simultaneously reduces storage costs and enables more complex neural architectures like Generative Recommenders.
fast.key_idea: Treating a user request (batch of impressions) as the atomic training unit instead of a single impression enables feature deduplication at the data layer and computation sharing at the model layer, unlocking efficiency gains across the entire training stack.
fast.why_it_matters: At industry scale, this paradigm reduces petabyte-scale storage requirements and training compute costs while enabling richer, more expressive architectures — meaning practitioners can build higher-quality recommendation models without proportionally higher infrastructure costs.
possible_use_cases: Large-scale e-commerce recommendation systems where a single search query generates many product impressions that share user context features
Social media feed ranking where a single app open triggers dozens of candidate posts sharing the same session-level user signals
Ad serving platforms seeking to reduce training data storage costs while scaling up sequence modeling architectures like Generative Recommenders

deep.method_breakdown: ROO redefines the atomic unit of training data from a single user impression (one item shown) to a full user request (all items shown in one session/page), enabling native deduplication of user context features that are shared across impressions within the same request. This co-design spans data logging (request-level storage instead of impression-level), the data pipeline (request-aware batching and processing), and model architecture (compute/communication deduplication across impressions in a request). The result is that user history and context embeddings are computed once per request rather than once per impression, unlocking capacity to scale up architectures like Generative Recommenders.
deep.key_innovation: Prior industry DLRMs treat each impression independently, causing redundant storage and computation for shared user context features across impressions in the same request. ROO's key innovation is elevating the request as the first-class data primitive, which simultaneously achieves: (1) lossless data compression via feature deduplication at logging time, (2) FLOPs reduction via shared computation across impressions, and (3) architectural unlock — the freed compute budget can be reinvested into more powerful per-request models (e.g., Generative Recommenders) that intrinsically benefit from seeing all impressions in a request together.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is treating compute deduplication not merely as an efficiency trick but as a way to change what architectures are feasible. Because user history sequences (long-tail, high-dimensional) are computed once per request rather than N times per N impressions, the effective FLOP budget per impression is reduced, making trillion-FLOP-scale architectures viable in production latency budgets. This works because user signals are genuinely shared across impressions in a request — the same user viewed all items — so deduplication is semantically lossless, not an approximation.
deep.limitations: ROO assumes impressions within a request share identical user context, which may not hold if user state updates between impressions (e.g., in streaming or real-time personalization settings). The paradigm requires significant infrastructure changes to data logging, storage, and serving pipelines, creating high migration cost for existing systems. Request-level batching can introduce load imbalance since request sizes vary; padding or bucketing strategies may reduce some efficiency gains. The paper focuses on offline training efficiency; online serving latency implications of request-level inference are not fully characterized.
interview_questions: Why does treating a request rather than an impression as the training unit enable feature deduplication, and what types of features benefit most?
How does ROO interact with Generative Recommenders specifically — why are GRs described as 'request-only friendly'?
What are the data pipeline engineering challenges when transitioning from impression-level to request-level data logging at petabyte scale?
How does ROO handle variable request sizes (different numbers of impressions per request) during training batch construction?
What are the tradeoffs between the storage savings from deduplication and any potential loss in training signal diversity or sample efficiency?
interview_answers: Within a single user request (e.g., one page load), user-side features — user ID embeddings, long user history sequences, demographic signals — are identical across all impressions. Under impression-level logging, these features are written redundantly for every item shown. Request-level logging writes them once and references them from each impression, achieving lossless compression. Item-side features are unique per impression and are not deduplicated. The highest benefit comes from long user history sequences, which are the most storage- and compute-intensive features in modern DLRMs.

Generative Recommenders model user interest as a generative process over item sequences and benefit from attending over all candidate items in a request jointly, rather than scoring each independently. Under impression-level training, a GR would need to independently encode the full user history for each impression — prohibitively expensive. ROO encodes user history once per request and shares it across all impression scoring heads, making GR-scale models tractable. Additionally, cross-impression attention (modeling which items in a request are shown together) is a natural fit for request-level architecture design.

Key challenges include: redesigning logging schemas so request boundaries are preserved and joinable at training time; ensuring that delayed feedback labels (clicks, conversions) can still be attributed back to request-level training examples; managing variable-length request records in distributed storage systems (e.g., handling serialization, sharding, and read amplification); and maintaining backward compatibility with existing impression-level A/B testing and evaluation infrastructure during the transition period.

Variable request sizes create non-uniform computation graphs, which complicate GPU batching. Common strategies include bucketing requests by impression count to minimize padding waste, or flattening to a two-level batch structure (request × max_impressions with masking). The choice affects GPU utilization — heavy padding wastes compute, while per-request dynamic shapes increase kernel launch overhead. Curriculum or importance-weighted sampling may also need to account for request size to avoid biasing the model toward large requests.

Deduplication reduces the number of unique training examples in a strict sense (fewer rows of data), but the information content is preserved because labels (impressions, clicks) remain at the item level. A potential concern is that request-level batching may reduce the effective diversity of user contexts seen per gradient step if large requests dominate. However, this is mitigated by the fact that ROO frees compute budget to scale up model capacity, which typically more than compensates for any marginal reduction in data diversity. The net effect on model quality is reported as positive in the paper.
